slides.title: Telling Timeless Tales
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 5 English, Unit 4: Telling Timeless Tales. In this unit you will explore myths and legends — the timeless tales that people have told for hundreds and even thousands of years. Myths are ancient stories that explain why things in nature happen (such as why the sun rises or why the sea is salty). They often involve gods, spirits, or supernatural beings. Legends are stories loosely based on real events or real people from history, but with unlikely or exaggerated details added over time. Both myths and legends have been passed down through oral tradition, meaning people told them aloud long before anyone wrote them down. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Explain the difference between a myth and a legend
Identify the key features of classic tales: oral tradition, timeless settings, moral or explanatory purpose, and larger-than-life characters
Use defining relative clauses to add essential information to your sentences
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to explain the difference between a myth and a legend. you will also identify the key features of classic tales: oral tradition, timeless settings, moral or explanatory purpose, and larger-than-life characters. you will also use defining relative clauses to add essential information to your sentences. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: hasty, reluctant, furious, anxious, grateful, irritable. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Telling Timeless Tales: source text 1 and Telling Timeless Tales: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: What Are Defining Relative Clauses?
bullets: A defining relative clause is a group of words that gives us essential information about a noun. It tells us exactly which person, place or thing we are...
slides.narration: The first language focus is What Are Defining Relative Clauses?. A defining relative clause is a group of words that gives us essential information about a noun. It tells us exactly which person, place or thing we are talking about. Without the clause, the sentence would be unclear or incomplete. Look at this example: Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Retell a Legend. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
